creator: Alexander Terp
description:
Surfaced during round-2 verification (2026-05-25), test 13b.

```rad
fn f(name: ["a", "b", "c"]):
    switch name:
        case "a":
            x = 1
        case "b":
            x = 2
        case "c":
            x = 3
        case "wat":   # <- this can never match; name is only ever "a", "b", or "c"
            x = 4
```

`./bin/radd check` reports no diagnostics. The `case "wat":` arm is unreachable because the discriminant's static type cannot produce "wat".

**Why it matters:** classic refactor stale-arm bug. Someone renamed an enum variant; the old name lingers in a switch and is silently dead code.

**Relationship to RAD40012:** the new unreachable-case detection (commit bd93a86e) catches duplicate keys *across arms within the switch*. This case is different: the key is unreachable because of the *discriminant's type*, not because of a prior arm.

**Fix shape:** during switch-walk, when the discriminant's narrowed type is a known enum (e.g. `TypingStrEnumT`), check each case key against the enum's value set. Keys outside the set fire RAD40012 (or a sibling code) with a message like:
"Case 'wat' is unreachable; 'name' is of type [\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"] and can never equal 'wat'"

**Snapshot test (per working principle):** the script above with the expected new diagnostic on the bad case.
title: Switch checker doesn't flag case-key not in the matched enum type
